response:
Based on what I can see, you have a Microsoft 401(k) account connected (MICROSOFT CORPORATION 401K 8216), which makes this a very relevant question. Since your financial profile doesn't yet include your salary or age details, I'll walk you through the framework clearly so you can plug in your own numbers.
The 2026 employee deferral limit
The IRS employee elective deferral limit for 2026 is $24,500 in pre-tax and/or Roth contributions. If you're age 50–59 or 64+, you can add an $8,000 catch-up (total $32,500), and if you happen to be ages 60–63, the "super catch-up" brings it to $35,750 — though only if your plan supports it.
Your contribution rate formula
Microsoft pays employees twice per month (24 paychecks). To spread the $24,500 evenly:
$24,500 ÷ 24 paychecks = approximately $1,021 per paycheck
To find your percentage, divide $24,500 by your annual base salary:
$100,000 salary → $24,500 ÷ $100,000 = 24.5%
$150,000 salary → $24,500 ÷ $150,000 = roughly 16–17%
$200,000 salary → $24,500 ÷ $200,000 = roughly 12–12.5%
$250,000 salary → $24,500 ÷ $250,000 = roughly 10%
How the Microsoft match works
Microsoft's formula is straightforward and generous: they match 50% of every pre-tax or Roth dollar you contribute, up to the $24,500 IRS regular deferral limit. That means:
Contribute the full $24,500 → Microsoft adds $12,250
Your total 401(k) funding for the year = $36,750
Vesting is immediate and 100% from day one — no waiting period
One important nuance: Microsoft does not match catch-up contributions (the extra $8,000 or $11,250 if you're 50+). The match applies only to the first $24,500 of regular deferrals. Also, Microsoft's match is always contributed as pre-tax, even if you elect Roth for your own contributions.
Mid-year adjustment (since we're already in June)
Since it's early June, you've used roughly 10 of your 24 paychecks, leaving approximately 14 paychecks in 2026. Here's how to recalibrate:
Check your year-to-date 401(k) contributions on Fidelity
Subtract that from $24,500 to find what remains
Divide the remainder by 14 (remaining paychecks)
Divide that per-paycheck dollar amount by your gross pay per check to get the new percentage
For example, if you've contributed $8,000 so far: $24,500 − $8,000 = $16,500 remaining ÷ 14 paychecks = $1,179 per paycheck. At a $150,000 salary, that's roughly $150,000 ÷ 24 = $6,250 per paycheck gross, so you'd set your rate to about $1,179 ÷ $6,250 ≈ 18.8% for the remainder of the year.
One practical note
Microsoft caps total combined 401(k) elections (pre-tax, Roth, and after-tax combined) at 65% of eligible compensation. At lower salary levels, this could limit your ability to max out, so it's worth checking your plan's summary plan description on the Fidelity portal.
Would you like me to run a retirement projection based on your specific salary and current 401(k) balance to show how maximizing the Microsoft match could compound over time?
Note: Investment calculations and projections are for illustrative purposes only. Market conditions change frequently. Consult a qualified financial advisor before making investment decisions.
Start Risk Assessment

Results aren't guaranteed. See disclosures.
